'''''Myc''''' is a family of [[regulator gene]]s and [[proto-oncogene]]s that [[Genetic code|code]] for [[transcription factor]]s. The ''Myc'' family consists of three related human genes: ''c-myc'' ([[MYC]]), ''l-myc'' ([[MYCL]]), and ''n-myc'' ([[N-Myc|MYCN]]). ''c-myc'' (also sometimes referred to as ''MYC'') was the first gene to be discovered in this family, due to [[#Discovery|homology with the viral gene ''v-myc'']].

In [[cancer]], ''c-myc'' is often [[Glossary of gene expression terms|constitutively]] (persistently) expressed. This leads to the increased expression of many genes, some of which are involved in [[cell proliferation]], contributing to the formation of cancer.<ref name="entrez">{{cite web | url = https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gene/17869 | title = Myc | publisher = NCBI }}</ref> A common human [[Chromosomal translocation|translocation]] involving ''c-myc'' is critical to the development of most cases of [[Burkitt lymphoma]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Finver SN, Nishikura K, Finger LR, Haluska FG, Finan J, Nowell PC, Croce CM | title = Sequence analysis of the ''Myc'' oncogene involved in the t(8;14)(q24;q11) chromosome translocation in a human leukemia T-cell line indicates that putative regulatory regions are not altered | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 85 | issue = 9 | pages = 3052–6 | date = May 1988 | pmid = 2834731 | pmc = 280141 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.85.9.3052 | bibcode = 1988PNAS...85.3052F | doi-access = free }}</ref> Constitutive upregulation of ''Myc'' genes have also been observed in carcinoma of the cervix, colon, breast, lung and stomach.<ref name="entrez" />

Myc is thus viewed as a promising target for anti-cancer drugs.<ref name="url_Reuters">{{cite web | url = https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-cancer-watson-idUSBRE90805N20130109 | title = DNA pioneer James Watson takes aim at cancer establishments | publisher = Reuters | author = Begley S | date = 2013-01-09 }}</ref> Unfortunately, Myc possesses several features that have rendered it difficult to drug to date, such that any anti-cancer drugs aimed at inhibiting Myc may continue to require perturbing the protein indirectly, such as by targeting the [[mRNA]] for the protein rather than via a [[small molecule]] that targets the protein itself.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Carabet LA, Rennie PS, Cherkasov A | title = Therapeutic Inhibition of Myc in Cancer. Structural Bases and Computer-Aided Drug Discovery Approaches | journal = International Journal of Molecular Sciences | volume = 20 | issue = 1 | date = December 2018 | pmid = 30597997 | pmc = 6337544 | doi = 10.3390/ijms20010120 | page=120| doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Dang CV, Reddy EP, Shokat KM, Soucek L | title = Drugging the 'undruggable' cancer targets | journal = Nature Reviews. Cancer | volume = 17 | issue = 8 | pages = 502–508 | date = August 2017 | pmid = 28643779 | pmc = 5945194 | doi = 10.1038/nrc.2017.36 | author-link4 = Laura Soucek }}</ref>

c-Myc also plays an important role in [[stem cell]] biology and was one of the original [[Yamanaka factors]] used to [[cellular reprogramming|reprogram]] [[somatic cells]] into [[induced pluripotent stem cells]].<ref name="pmid23021216">{{cite journal | vauthors = Nie Z, Hu G, Wei G, Cui K, Yamane A, Resch W, Wang R, Green DR, Tessarollo L, Casellas R, Zhao K, Levens D | title = c-Myc is a universal amplifier of expressed genes in lymphocytes and embryonic stem cells | journal = Cell | volume = 151 | issue = 1 | pages = 68–79 | date = September 2012 | pmid = 23021216 | pmc = 3471363 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2012.08.033 }}</ref>

In the [[human genome]], ''c-myc'' is located on [[Chromosome 8 (human)|chromosome 8]] and is believed to regulate expression of 15% of all genes<ref name="pmid17928593">{{cite journal | vauthors = Gearhart J, Pashos EE, Prasad MK | title = Pluripotency redux--advances in stem-cell research | journal = The New England Journal of Medicine | volume = 357 | issue = 15 | pages = 1469–72 | date = October 2007 | pmid = 17928593 | doi = 10.1056/NEJMp078126 }}</ref> through binding on enhancer box sequences ([[E-box]]es).

In addition to its role as a classical transcription factor, ''N-myc'' may recruit [[histone acetyltransferase]]s (HATs). This allows it to regulate global chromatin structure via histone acetylation.<ref name="pmid19047142">{{cite journal | vauthors = Cotterman R, Jin VX, Krig SR, Lemen JM, Wey A, Farnham PJ, Knoepfler PS | title = N-Myc regulates a widespread euchromatic program in the human genome partially independent of its role as a classical transcription factor | journal = Cancer Research | volume = 68 | issue = 23 | pages = 9654–62 | date = December 2008 | pmid = 19047142 | pmc = 2637654 | doi = 10.1158/0008-5472.CAN-08-1961 }}</ref>

==Discovery==

The ''Myc'' family was first established after discovery of [[Homology (biology)|homology]] between an oncogene carried by the Avian '''v'''irus, '''My'''elo'''c'''ytomatosis (''v-myc''; {{UniProt|P10395}}) and a human gene over-expressed in various cancers, '''c'''ellular Myc (''c-Myc'').{{citation needed|date=June 2020}} Later, discovery of further homologous genes in humans led to the addition of ''n-Myc'' and ''l-Myc'' to the family of genes.<ref name="Wolf">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wolf E, Eilers M | title=Targeting MYC Proteins for Tumor Therapy| year=2020 | journal=Annual Review of Cancer Biology| volume=4| pages=61–75 |doi=10.1146/annurev-cancerbio-030518-055826 | doi-access=free}}</ref>

The most frequently discussed example of ''c-Myc'' as a proto-oncogene is its implication in [[Burkitt's lymphoma]]. In Burkitt's lymphoma, cancer cells show [[chromosomal translocation]]s, most commonly between [[chromosome 8]] and [[chromosome 14]] [t(8;14)]. This causes ''c-Myc'' to be placed downstream of the highly active immunoglobulin (Ig) promoter region, leading to overexpression of ''Myc''.

==Structure==

The protein products of ''Myc'' family genes all belong to the Myc family of transcription factors, which contain [[bHLH]] (basic helix-loop-helix) and LZ ([[leucine zipper]]) structural motifs.  The bHLH motif allows Myc proteins to bind with [[DNA]], while the leucine zipper TF-binding motif allows dimerization with [[MAX (gene)|Max]], another bHLH transcription factor.

Myc [[mRNA]] contains an [[Internal ribosome entry site|IRES]] (internal ribosome entry site) that allows the RNA to be translated into protein when [[5' cap]]-dependent translation is inhibited, such as during viral infection.

== Function ==

Myc proteins are [[transcription factor]]s that activate expression of many pro-proliferative genes through binding enhancer box sequences ([[E-box]]es) and recruiting [[histone acetyltransferase]]s (HATs). Myc is thought to function by upregulating transcript elongation of actively transcribed genes through the recruitment of [[transcriptional elongation]] factors.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rahl PB, Young RA | title = MYC and transcription elongation | journal = Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Medicine | volume = 4 | issue = 1 | article-number = a020990 | date = January 2014 | pmid = 24384817 | pmc = 3869279 | doi = 10.1101/cshperspect.a020990 }}</ref> It can also act as a transcriptional repressor. By binding Miz-1 transcription factor and displacing the [[EP300|p300]] [[co-activator]], it inhibits expression of Miz-1 target genes. In addition, myc has a direct role in the control of DNA replication.<ref name="pmid17597761">{{cite journal | vauthors = Dominguez-Sola D, Ying CY, Grandori C, Ruggiero L, Chen B, Li M, Galloway DA, Gu W, Gautier J, Dalla-Favera R | title = Non-transcriptional control of DNA replication by c-Myc | journal = Nature | volume = 448 | issue = 7152 | pages = 445–51 | date = July 2007 | pmid = 17597761 | doi = 10.1038/nature05953 | bibcode = 2007Natur.448..445D | s2cid = 4422771 }}</ref> This activity could contribute to DNA amplification in cancer cells.<ref name="pmid1886715">{{cite journal | vauthors = Denis N, Kitzis A, Kruh J, Dautry F, Corcos D | title = Stimulation of methotrexate resistance and dihydrofolate reductase gene amplification by c-myc | journal = Oncogene | volume = 6 | issue = 8 | pages = 1453–7 | date = August 1991 | pmid = 1886715 }}</ref>

Myc is activated upon various [[mitogen|mitogenic signal]]s such as serum stimulation or by [[Wnt signalling pathway|Wnt]], [[Sonic hedgehog|Shh]] and [[Epidermal growth factor|EGF]] (via the [[MAPK/ERK pathway]]).<ref name="pmid6692471">{{cite journal | vauthors = Campisi J, Gray HE, Pardee AB, Dean M, Sonenshein GE | title = Cell-cycle control of c-myc but not c-ras expression is lost following chemical transformation | journal = Cell | volume = 36 | issue = 2 | pages = 241–7 | year = 1984 | pmid = 6692471 | doi = 10.1016/0092-8674(84)90217-4 | s2cid = 29661004 }}</ref>
By modifying the expression of its target genes, Myc activation results in numerous biological effects. The first to be discovered was its capability to drive [[cell proliferation]] (upregulates cyclins, downregulates p21), but it also plays a very important role in regulating [[cell growth]] (upregulates ribosomal RNA and proteins), [[apoptosis]] (downregulates [[Bcl-2]]), differentiation, and [[stem cell]] self-renewal. Nucleotide metabolism genes are upregulated by Myc,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Liu YC, Li F, Handler J, Huang CR, Xiang Y, Neretti N, Sedivy JM, Zeller KI, Dang CV | title = Global regulation of nucleotide biosynthetic genes by c-Myc | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 3 | issue = 7 | article-number = e2722 | date = July 2008 | pmid = 18628958 | pmc = 2444028 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0002722 | bibcode = 2008PLoSO...3.2722L | doi-access = free }}</ref> which are necessary for Myc induced proliferation<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mannava S, Grachtchouk V, Wheeler LJ, Im M, Zhuang D, Slavina EG, Mathews CK, Shewach DS, Nikiforov MA | title = Direct role of nucleotide metabolism in C-MYC-dependent proliferation of melanoma cells | journal = Cell Cycle | volume = 7 | issue = 15 | pages = 2392–400 | date = August 2008 | pmid = 18677108 | pmc = 3744895 | doi = 10.4161/cc.6390 }}</ref> or cell growth.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Aughey GN, Grice SJ, Liu JL | title = The Interplay between Myc and CTP Synthase in Drosophila | journal = PLOS Genetics | volume = 12 | issue = 2 | article-number = e1005867 | date = February 2016 | pmid = 26889675 | pmc = 4759343 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pgen.1005867 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

There have been several studies that have clearly indicated Myc's role in cell competition.<ref name="pmid23842495">{{cite journal | vauthors = Clavería C, Giovinazzo G, Sierra R, Torres M | title = Myc-driven endogenous cell competition in the early mammalian embryo | journal = Nature | volume = 500 | issue = 7460 | pages = 39–44 | date = August 2013 | pmid = 23842495 | doi = 10.1038/nature12389 | bibcode = 2013Natur.500...39C | s2cid = 4414411 }}</ref>

A major effect of c-myc is [[B cell]] proliferation, and gain of MYC has been associated with B cell malignancies and their increased aggressiveness, including histological transformation.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = de Alboran IM, O'Hagan RC, Gärtner F, Malynn B, Davidson L, Rickert R, Rajewsky K, DePinho RA, Alt FW | title = Analysis of C-MYC function in normal cells via conditional gene-targeted mutation | journal = Immunity | volume = 14 | issue = 1 | pages = 45–55 | date = January 2001 | pmid = 11163229 | doi = 10.1016/S1074-7613(01)00088-7 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In B cells, Myc acts as a classical oncogene by regulating a number of pro-proliferative and anti-apoptotic pathways, this also includes tuning of BCR signaling and CD40 signaling in regulation of microRNAs (miR-29, miR-150, miR-17-92).<ref name=pmid18423194>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mendell JT | title = miRiad roles for the miR-17-92 cluster in development and disease | journal = Cell | volume = 133 | issue = 2 | pages = 217–22 | date = April 2008 | pmid = 18423194 | pmc = 2732113 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2008.04.001 }}</ref>

c-Myc induces [[MTDH]] (AEG-1) gene expression and in turn itself requires AEG-1 oncogene for its expression.

== Myc-nick ==
[[File:Myc-Nick.pdf|Myc-Nick|thumb|right|upright=1.5]]
Myc-nick is a cytoplasmic form of Myc produced by a partial proteolytic cleavage of full-length c-Myc and N-Myc.<ref name="pmid20691906">{{cite journal | vauthors = Conacci-Sorrell M, Ngouenet C, Eisenman RN | title = Myc-nick: a cytoplasmic cleavage product of Myc that promotes alpha-tubulin acetylation and cell differentiation | journal = Cell | volume = 142 | issue = 3 | pages = 480–93 | date = August 2010 | pmid = 20691906 | pmc = 2923036 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2010.06.037 }}</ref> Myc cleavage is mediated by the [[calpain]] family of calcium-dependent cytosolic proteases.

The cleavage of Myc by calpains is a constitutive process but is enhanced under conditions that require rapid downregulation of Myc levels, such as during terminal differentiation. Upon cleavage, the [[C-terminus]] of Myc (containing the [[DNA binding domain]]) is degraded, while Myc-nick, the [[N-terminal]] segment 298-residue segment remains in the [[cytoplasm]]. Myc-nick contains binding domains for [[histone acetyltransferase]]s and for [[ubiquitin ligase]]s.

The functions of Myc-nick are currently under investigation, but this new Myc family member was found to regulate cell morphology, at least in part, by interacting with [[acyltransferase|acetyl transferases]] to promote the acetylation of [[α-tubulin]]. [[Ectopic expression]] of Myc-nick accelerates the differentiation of committed [[myoblast]]s into muscle cells.

== Clinical significance ==
A large body of evidence shows that Myc genes and proteins are highly relevant for treating tumors.<ref name="Wolf"/> Except for early response genes, Myc universally upregulates gene expression.  Furthermore, the upregulation is nonlinear.  Genes for which expression is already significantly upregulated in the absence of Myc are strongly boosted in the presence of Myc, whereas genes for which expression is low in the absence Myc get only a small boost when Myc is present.<ref name="pmid23021216"/>

Inactivation of SUMO-activating enzyme ([[SAE1]] / [[UBA2|SAE2]]) in the presence of Myc hyperactivation results in mitotic catastrophe and cell death in cancer cells.  Hence inhibitors of [[SUMOylation]] may be a possible treatment for cancer.<ref name="pmid22157079">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kessler JD, Kahle KT, Sun T, Meerbrey KL, Schlabach MR, Schmitt EM, Skinner SO, Xu Q, Li MZ, Hartman ZC, Rao M, Yu P, Dominguez-Vidana R, Liang AC, Solimini NL, Bernardi RJ, Yu B, Hsu T, Golding I, Luo J, Osborne CK, Creighton CJ, [[Susan Hilsenbeck|Hilsenbeck SG]], Schiff R, Shaw CA, Elledge SJ, Westbrook TF | title = A SUMOylation-dependent transcriptional subprogram is required for Myc-driven tumorigenesis | journal = Science | volume = 335 | issue = 6066 | pages = 348–53 | date = January 2012 | pmid = 22157079 | pmc = 4059214 | doi = 10.1126/science.1212728 | bibcode = 2012Sci...335..348K }}</ref>

Amplification of the MYC gene was found in a significant number of epithelial [[ovarian cancer]] cases.<ref name="pmid23791828">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ross JS, Ali SM, Wang K, Palmer G, Yelensky R, Lipson D, Miller VA, Zajchowski D, Shawver LK, Stephens PJ | title = Comprehensive genomic profiling of epithelial ovarian cancer by next generation sequencing-based diagnostic assay reveals new routes to targeted therapies | journal = Gynecologic Oncology | volume = 130 | issue = 3 | pages = 554–9 | date = September 2013 | pmid = 23791828 | doi = 10.1016/j.ygyno.2013.06.019 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In TCGA datasets, the amplification of Myc occurs in several cancer types, including breast, colorectal, pancreatic, gastric, and uterine cancers.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chen Y, McGee J, Chen X, Doman TN, Gong X, Zhang Y, Hamm N, Ma X, Higgs RE, Bhagwat SV, Buchanan S, Peng SB, Staschke KA, Yadav V, Yue Y, Kouros-Mehr H | title = Identification of druggable cancer driver genes amplified across TCGA datasets | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 9 | issue = 5 | article-number = e98293 | date = 2014 | pmid = 24874471 | pmc = 4038530 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0098293 | bibcode = 2014PLoSO...998293C | doi-access = free }}</ref>

In the experimental transformation process of normal cells into cancer cells, the MYC gene can cooperate with the RAS gene.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Land H, Parada LF, Weinberg RA | title = Tumorigenic conversion of primary embryo fibroblasts requires at least two cooperating oncogenes | journal = Nature | volume = 304 | issue = 5927 | pages = 596–602 | year = 1983 | pmid = 6308472 | doi = 10.1038/304596a0 | bibcode = 1983Natur.304..596L | s2cid = 2338865 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Radner H, el-Shabrawi Y, Eibl RH, Brüstle O, Kenner L, Kleihues P, Wiestler OD | title = Tumor induction by ras and myc oncogenes in fetal and neonatal brain: modulating effects of developmental stage and retroviral dose | journal = Acta Neuropathologica | volume = 86 | issue = 5 | pages = 456–65 | year = 1993 | pmid = 8310796 | doi = 10.1007/bf00228580 | s2cid = 2972931 }}</ref>

Expression of Myc is highly dependent on [[BRD4]] function in some cancers.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fowler T, Ghatak P, Price DH, Conaway R, Conaway J, Chiang CM, Bradner JE, Shilatifard A, Roy AL | title = Regulation of MYC expression and differential JQ1 sensitivity in cancer cells | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 9 | issue = 1 | article-number = e87003 | year = 2014 | pmid = 24466310 | pmc = 3900694 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0087003 | bibcode = 2014PLoSO...987003F | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Shi J, Vakoc CR | title = The mechanisms behind the therapeutic activity of BET bromodomain inhibition | journal = Molecular Cell | volume = 54 | issue = 5 | pages = 728–36 | date = June 2014 | pmid = 24905006 | pmc = 4236231 | doi = 10.1016/j.molcel.2014.05.016 }}</ref> [[BET inhibitors]] have been used to successfully block Myc function in pre-clinical cancer models and are currently being evaluated in clinical trials.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Delmore JE, Issa GC, Lemieux ME, Rahl PB, Shi J, Jacobs HM, Kastritis E, Gilpatrick T, Paranal RM, Qi J, Chesi M, Schinzel AC, McKeown MR, Heffernan TP, Vakoc CR, Bergsagel PL, Ghobrial IM, Richardson PG, Young RA, Hahn WC, Anderson KC, Kung AL, Bradner JE, Mitsiades CS | title = BET bromodomain inhibition as a therapeutic strategy to target c-Myc | journal = Cell | volume = 146 | issue = 6 | pages = 904–17 | date = September 2011 | pmid = 21889194 | pmc = 3187920 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2011.08.017 }}</ref>

MYC expression is controlled by a wide variety of noncoding RNAs, including [[miRNA]], [[lncRNA]], and [[circRNA]]. Some of these RNAs have been shown to be specific for certain types of human tissues and tumors.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stasevich EM, Murashko MM, Zinevich LS, Demin DE, Schwartz AM| title = The Role of Non-Coding RNAs in the Regulation of the Proto-Oncogene MYC in Different Types of Cancer | journal = Biomedicines | volume = 9 | issue = 8 | page = 921 | date = July 2021 | pmid = 34440124| pmc =  8389562| doi = 10.3390/biomedicines9080921| doi-access = free }}</ref> Changes in the expression of such RNAs can potentially be used to develop targeted tumor therapy.

== MYC rearrangements ==
''MYC'' [[Chromosomal rearrangement|chromosomal rearrangements]] (''MYC''-R) occur in 10% to 15% of [[diffuse large B-cell lymphoma]] (DLBCLs), an aggressive [[Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma]] (NHL). Patients with ''MYC''-R have inferior outcomes and can be classified as single-hit, when they only have ''MYC''-R; as double hit when the rearrangement is accompanied by a translocation of [[Bcl-2|''BCL2'']] or ''[[BCL6]]''; and as triple hit when ''MYC''-R includes both ''BCL2'' and ''BCL6''. Double and triple hit lymphoma have been recently classified as high-grade B-cell lymphoma (HGBCL) and it is associated with a poor prognosis.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Eertink |first1=Jakoba J. |last2=Zwezerijnen |first2=Gerben J. C. |last3=Wiegers |first3=Sanne E. |last4=Pieplenbosch |first4=Simone |last5=Chamuleau |first5=Martine E. D. |last6=Lugtenburg |first6=Pieternella J. |last7=de Jong |first7=Daphne |last8=Ylstra |first8=Bauke |last9=Mendeville |first9=Matias |last10=Dührsen |first10=Ulrich |last11=Hanoun |first11=Christine |last12=Hüttmann |first12=Andreas |last13=Richter |first13=Julia |last14=Klapper |first14=Wolfram |last15=Jauw |first15=Yvonne W. S. |last16=Hoekstra |first16=Otto S. |last17=de Vet |first17=Henrica C. W. |last18=Boellaard |first18=Ronald |last19=Zijlstra |first19=Josée M. |title=Baseline radiomics features and MYC rearrangement status predict progression in aggressive B-cell lymphoma |journal=Blood Advances |date=24 January 2023 |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=214–223 |doi=10.1182/bloodadvances.2022008629 |pmid=36306337 |pmc=9841040 }}</ref>

''MYC''-R in DLBCL/HGBCL is believed to arise through the aberrant activity of activation-induced cytidine deaminase (AICDA), which facilitates somatic hypermutation (SHM) and class-switch recombination (CSR).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lieber |first1=Michael R. |title=Mechanisms of human lymphoid chromosomal translocations |journal=Nature Reviews Cancer |date=June 2016 |volume=16 |issue=6 |pages=387–398 |doi=10.1038/nrc.2016.40 |pmid=27220482 |pmc=5336345 }}</ref> Although AICDA primarily targets IG loci for SHM and CSR, its off-target mutagenic effects can impact lymphoma-associated oncogenes like ''MYC'', potentially leading to oncogenic rearrangements. The breakpoints in ''MYC'' rearrangements show considerable variability within the ''MYC'' region. These breakpoints may occur within the so-called "genic cluster," a region spanning approximately 1.5 kb upstream of the transcription start site, as well as the first exon and intron of ''MYC''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chong |first1=Lauren C. |last2=Ben-Neriah |first2=Susana |last3=Slack |first3=Graham W. |last4=Freeman |first4=Ciara |last5=Ennishi |first5=Daisuke |last6=Mottok |first6=Anja |last7=Collinge |first7=Brett |last8=Abrisqueta |first8=Pau |last9=Farinha |first9=Pedro |last10=Boyle |first10=Merrill |last11=Meissner |first11=Barbara |last12=Kridel |first12=Robert |last13=Gerrie |first13=Alina S. |last14=Villa |first14=Diego |last15=Savage |first15=Kerry J. |last16=Sehn |first16=Laurie H. |last17=Siebert |first17=Reiner |last18=Morin |first18=Ryan D. |last19=Gascoyne |first19=Randy D. |last20=Marra |first20=Marco A. |last21=Connors |first21=Joseph M. |last22=Mungall |first22=Andrew J. |last23=Steidl |first23=Christian |last24=Scott |first24=David W. |title=High-resolution architecture and partner genes of MYC rearrangements in lymphoma with DLBCL morphology |journal=Blood Advances |date=22 October 2018 |volume=2 |issue=20 |pages=2755–2765 |doi=10.1182/bloodadvances.2018023572 |pmid=30348671 |pmc=6199666 }}</ref> 

[[Fluorescence in situ hybridization|Fluorescence ''in situ'' hybridization]] (FISH) has become a routine practice in many clinical laboratories for lymphoma characterization. A break-apart (BAP) FISH probe is commonly utilized for the detection of ''MYC''-R due to the variability of breakpoints in the ''MYC'' locus and the diversity of rearrangement partners, including immunoglobulin (IG) and non-IG partners (i.e. ''BCL2''/''BCL6''). The ''MYC'' BAP probe includes a red and a green probe which hybridize 5' and 3' to the ''MYC'' gen, respectively. In an intact ''MYC'' locus, these probes yield a fusion signal. When ''MYC''-R occur, two types of signals can be observed:

* Balanced patterns: These patterns present separate red and green signals.

* Unbalanced patterns: When isolated red or green signals in the absence of the corresponding green or red signal is observed. Unbalanced ''MYC''-R are frequently associated with increased ''MYC'' expression.

There is a large variability in the interpretation of unbalanced ''MYC'' BAP results among the scientists, which can impact diagnostic classification and therapeutic management of the patients.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rosenwald |first1=Andreas |last2=Bens |first2=Susanne |last3=Advani |first3=Ranjana |last4=Barrans |first4=Sharon |last5=Copie-Bergman |first5=Christiane |last6=Elsensohn |first6=Mad-Helenie |last7=Natkunam |first7=Yaso |last8=Calaminici |first8=Maria |last9=Sander |first9=Birgitta |last10=Baia |first10=Maryse |last11=Smith |first11=Alexandra |last12=Painter |first12=Daniel |last13=Pham |first13=Luu |last14=Zhao |first14=Shuchun |last15=Ziepert |first15=Marita |last16=Jordanova |first16=Ekaterina S. |last17=Molina |first17=Thierry J. |last18=Kersten |first18=Marie José |last19=Kimby |first19=Eva |last20=Klapper |first20=Wolfram |last21=Raemaekers |first21=John |last22=Schmitz |first22=Norbert |last23=Jardin |first23=Fabrice |last24=Stevens |first24=Wendy B.C. |last25=Hoster |first25=Eva |last26=Hagenbeek |first26=Anton |last27=Gribben |first27=John G. |last28=Siebert |first28=Reiner |last29=Gascoyne |first29=Randy D. |last30=Scott |first30=David W. |last31=Gaulard |first31=Philippe |last32=Salles |first32=Gilles |last33=Burton |first33=Catherine |last34=de Jong |first34=Daphne |last35=Sehn |first35=Laurie H. |last36=Maucort-Boulch |first36=Delphine |title=Prognostic Significance of MYC Rearrangement and Translocation Partner in Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: A Study by the Lunenburg Lymphoma Biomarker Consortium |journal=Journal of Clinical Oncology |date=10 December 2019 |volume=37 |issue=35 |pages=3359–3368 |doi=10.1200/JCO.19.00743 |pmid=31498031 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gagnon |first1=Marie-France |last2=Penheiter |first2=Alan R. |last3=Harris |first3=Faye |last4=Sadeghian |first4=Dorsay |last5=Johnson |first5=Sarah H. |last6=Karagouga |first6=Giannoula |last7=McCune |first7=Alexa |last8=Zepeda-Mendoza |first8=Cinthya |last9=Greipp |first9=Patricia T. |last10=Xu |first10=Xinjie |last11=Ketterling |first11=Rhett P. |last12=McPhail |first12=Ellen D. |last13=King |first13=Rebecca L. |last14=Peterson |first14=Jess F. |last15=Vasmatzis |first15=George |last16=Baughn |first16=Linda B. |title=Unraveling the genomic underpinnings of unbalanced MYC break-apart FISH results using whole genome sequencing analysis |journal=Blood Cancer Journal |date=19 December 2023 |volume=13 |issue=1 |page=190 |doi=10.1038/s41408-023-00967-8 |pmid=38114462 |pmc=10730864 }}</ref>

== Animal models ==
In [[Drosophila melanogaster|Drosophila]] Myc is encoded by the diminutive locus, (which was known to geneticists prior to 1935).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Slizynska H | title = Salivary Chromosome Analysis of the White-Facet Region of Drosophila Melanogaster | journal = Genetics | volume = 23 | issue = 3 | pages = 291–9 | date = May 1938 | doi = 10.1093/genetics/23.3.291 | pmid = 17246888 | pmc = 1209013 }}</ref> Classical diminutive alleles resulted in a viable animal with small body size. Drosophila has subsequently been used to implicate Myc in cell competition,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = de la Cova C, Abril M, Bellosta P, Gallant P, Johnston LA | title = Drosophila myc regulates organ size by inducing cell competition | journal = Cell | volume = 117 | issue = 1 | pages = 107–16 | date = April 2004 | pmid = 15066286 | doi = 10.1016/S0092-8674(04)00214-4 | s2cid = 18357397 | doi-access = free }}</ref> endoreplication,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Maines JZ, Stevens LM, Tong X, Stein D | title = Drosophila dMyc is required for ovary cell growth and endoreplication | journal = Development | volume = 131 | issue = 4 | pages = 775–86 | date = February 2004 | pmid = 14724122 | doi = 10.1242/dev.00932 | s2cid = 721144 | doi-access =  }}</ref> and cell growth.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Johnston LA, Prober DA, Edgar BA, Eisenman RN, Gallant P | title = Drosophila myc regulates cellular growth during development | journal = Cell | volume = 98 | issue = 6 | pages = 779–90 | date = September 1999 | pmid = 10499795 | doi = 10.1016/S0092-8674(00)81512-3 | pmc = 10176494 | s2cid = 5215149 | url = https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/740/1/Johnston_1999.pdf | access-date = 2020-03-20 | archive-date = 2022-04-03 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220403025727/https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/740/1/Johnston_1999.pdf }}</ref>

During the discovery of Myc gene, it was realized that chromosomes that reciprocally translocate to chromosome 8 contained [[immunoglobulin]] genes at the break-point. To study the mechanism of tumorigenesis in Burkitt lymphoma by mimicking expression pattern of Myc in these cancer cells, transgenic mouse models were developed. Myc gene placed under the control of [[IgM]] heavy chain enhancer in transgenic mice gives rise to mainly lymphomas. Later on, in order to study effects of Myc in other types of cancer, transgenic mice that overexpress Myc in different tissues (liver, breast) were also made. In all these mouse models overexpression of Myc causes tumorigenesis, illustrating the potency of Myc oncogene.
In a study with mice, reduced expression of Myc was shown to induce longevity, with significantly extended median and maximum lifespans in both sexes and a reduced mortality rate across all ages, better health, cancer progression was slower, better metabolism and they had smaller bodies. Also, Less TOR, AKT, S6K and other changes in energy and metabolic pathways (such as AMPK, more oxygen consumption, more body movements, etc.).  The study by John M. Sedivy and others used Cre-Loxp -recombinase to knockout one copy of Myc and this resulted in a "Haplo-insufficient" genotype noted as Myc±. The phenotypes seen oppose the effects of normal aging and are shared with many other long-lived mouse models such as CR (calorie restriction) ames dwarf, rapamycin, metformin and resveratrol. One study found that Myc and [[p53]] genes were key to the survival of [[chronic myeloid leukaemia]] (CML) cells. Targeting Myc and p53 proteins with drugs gave positive results on mice with CML.<ref>{{cite journal|display-authors=6|vauthors=Abraham SA, Hopcroft LE, Carrick E, Drotar ME, Dunn K, Williamson AJ, Korfi K, Baquero P, Park LE, Scott MT, Pellicano F, Pierce A, Copland M, Nourse C, Grimmond SM, Vetrie D, Whetton AD, Holyoake TL|date=June 2016|title=Dual targeting of p53 and c-MYC selectively eliminates leukaemic stem cells|url=http://man.ac.uk/gIYpq4|journal=Nature|volume=534|issue=7607|pages=341–6|doi=10.1038/nature18288|pmc=4913876|pmid=27281222|bibcode=2016Natur.534..341A}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.myscience.org.uk/news/2016/cientists_identify_drugs_to_target_achilles_heel_of_chronic_myeloid_leukaemia_cells-2016-glasgow|title=Scientists identify drugs to target 'Achilles heel' of Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia cells|date=2016-06-08|website=myScience|access-date=2016-06-09|archive-date=2018-07-27|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180727212225/https://www.myscience.org.uk/news/2016/cientists_identify_drugs_to_target_achilles_heel_of_chronic_myeloid_leukaemia_cells-2016-glasgow}}</ref>

==Relationship to stem cells==

''Myc'' genes play a number of normal roles in stem cells including pluripotent stem cells. In neural stem cells, N-Myc promotes a rapidly proliferative stem cell and precursor-like state in the developing brain, while inhibiting differentiation.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Knoepfler PS, Cheng PF, Eisenman RN | title = N-myc is essential during neurogenesis for the rapid expansion of progenitor cell populations and the inhibition of neuronal differentiation | journal = Genes & Development | volume = 16 | issue = 20 | pages = 2699–712 | date = October 2002 | pmid = 12381668 | doi = 10.1101/gad.1021202 | pmc = 187459 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In hematopoietic stem cells, Myc controls the balance between self-renewal and differentiation.<ref name="c-Myc controls the balance between">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wilson A, Murphy MJ, Oskarsson T, Kaloulis K, Bettess MD, Oser GM, Pasche AC, Knabenhans C, Macdonald HR, Trumpp A | display-authors = 6 | title = c-Myc controls the balance between hematopoietic stem cell self-renewal and differentiation | journal = Genes & Development | volume = 18 | issue = 22 | pages = 2747–63 | date = November 2004 | pmid = 15545632 | doi = 10.1101/gad.313104 | pmc = 528895 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In particular, long-term hematopoietic stem cells (LT-HSCs) express low levels of c-Myc, ensuring self-renewal. Enforced expression of c-Myc in LT-HSCs promotes differentiation at the expense of self-renewal, resulting in stem cell exhaustion.<ref name="c-Myc controls the balance between"/> In pathological states and specifically in acute myeloid leukemia, oxidant stress can trigger higher levels of Myc expression that affects the behavior of leukemia stem cells.<ref name="pmid38201575">{{cite journal | vauthors = Vlahopoulos S, Pan L, Varisli L, Dancik GM, Karantanos T, Boldogh I | title = OGG1 as an Epigenetic Reader Affects NFκB: What This Means for Cancer | journal = Cancers | volume = 16 | issue = 1 | page = 148 | date = Dec 2023 | pmid = 38201575 | doi = 10.3390/cancers16010148| doi-access = free | pmc = 10778025 }}</ref>

''c-Myc'' plays a major role in the generation of [[induced pluripotent stem cell]]s (iPSCs). It is one of the original factors discovered by Yamanaka et al. to encourage cells to return to a 'stem-like' state alongside transcription factors [[Oct4]], [[Sox2]] and [[Klf4]]. It has since been shown that it is possible to generate iPSCs without ''c-Myc''.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Takahashi K, Yamanaka S | title = A decade of transcription factor-mediated reprogramming to pluripotency | journal = Nature Reviews. Molecular Cell Biology | volume = 17 | issue = 3 | pages = 183–93 | date = March 2016 | pmid = 26883003 | doi = 10.1038/nrm.2016.8 | s2cid = 7593915 }}</ref>

== Interactions ==

Myc has been shown to [[Protein-protein interaction|interact]] with:

* [[ACTL6A]]<ref name = pmid11839798/>
* [[BRCA1]]<ref name = pmid11916966/><ref name = pmid14612409>{{cite journal | vauthors = Xiong J, Fan S, Meng Q, Schramm L, Wang C, Bouzahza B, Zhou J, Zafonte B, Goldberg ID, Haddad BR, Pestell RG, Rosen EM | title = BRCA1 inhibition of telomerase activity in cultured cells | journal = Molecular and Cellular Biology | volume = 23 | issue = 23 | pages = 8668–90 | date = December 2003 | pmid = 14612409 | pmc = 262673 | doi = 10.1128/mcb.23.23.8668-8690.2003 }}</ref><ref name = pmid12646176>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zhou C, Liu J | title = Inhibition of human telomerase reverse transcriptase gene expression by BRCA1 in human ovarian cancer cells | journal = Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications | volume = 303 | issue = 1 | pages = 130–6 | date = March 2003 | pmid = 12646176 | doi = 10.1016/s0006-291x(03)00318-8 | bibcode = 2003BBRC..303..130Z }}</ref><ref name = pmid9788437>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wang Q, Zhang H, Kajino K, Greene MI | title = BRCA1 binds c-Myc and inhibits its transcriptional and transforming activity in cells | journal = Oncogene | volume = 17 | issue = 15 | pages = 1939–48 | date = October 1998 | pmid = 9788437 | doi = 10.1038/sj.onc.1202403 | s2cid = 30771256 | doi-access =  }}</ref>

* [[PCAF]]<ref name = pmid12660246>{{cite journal | vauthors = Liu X, Tesfai J, Evrard YA, Dent SY, Martinez E | title = c-Myc transformation domain recruits the human STAGA complex and requires TRRAP and GCN5 acetylase activity for transcription activation | journal = The Journal of Biological Chemistry | volume = 278 | issue = 22 | pages = 20405–12 | date = May 2003 | pmid = 12660246 | pmc = 4031917 | doi = 10.1074/jbc.M211795200 | doi-access = free }}</ref>
* [[PFDN5]]<ref name = pmid9792694>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mori K, Maeda Y, Kitaura H, Taira T, Iguchi-Ariga SM, Ariga H | title = MM-1, a novel c-Myc-associating protein that represses transcriptional activity of c-Myc | journal = The Journal of Biological Chemistry | volume = 273 | issue = 45 | pages = 29794–800 | date = November 1998 | pmid = 9792694 | doi = 10.1074/jbc.273.45.29794 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name = pmid11567024>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fujioka Y, Taira T, Maeda Y, Tanaka S, Nishihara H, Iguchi-Ariga SM, Nagashima K, Ariga H | title = MM-1, a c-Myc-binding protein, is a candidate for a tumor suppressor in leukemia/lymphoma and tongue cancer | journal = The Journal of Biological Chemistry | volume = 276 | issue = 48 | pages = 45137–44 | date = November 2001 | pmid = 11567024 | doi = 10.1074/jbc.M106127200 | doi-access =  free}}</ref>
